target.name: Rehmannin B
target.motivation: Rehmannins B-D possess a novel tricyclic ring system bearing five contiguous stereocenters on the heterocyclic core together with a unique amide group on the long chain, rendering them attractive targets for total synthesis. Compound 3 (Rehmannin C) exhibited inhibition of NO secretion in LPS-induced RAW264.7 cells, suggesting anti-inflammatory activity.

success_criteria.free_text: Achieve the correct absolute configuration (4R, 5R, 6S, 7R, and 8S) for the tricyclic core.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The paper focuses on the isolation, structural elucidation, proposed biogenetic pathway, and bioactive assessment of rehmannins A-D, not their total synthesis. The authors mention that rehmannins B-D are 'attractive targets for total synthesis' but do not describe any synthetic efforts or strategies in this paper.

thought.background: Rehmannia glutinosa is a valuable medicinal herb in Traditional Chinese Medicine. Many secondary metabolites have been isolated, but the pigments responsible for its characteristic yellow color remained unidentified.
thought.gap: Identify the specific chromogenic compounds responsible for the yellow-orange pigments in R. glutinosa roots and explore potential new carotenoid-derived metabolites.
thought.decision: To address this gap, we focused on the pigmented tissues of R. glutinosa roots to isolate and characterize novel compounds.

outcome.observation: Successfully isolated four novel pigmented carotenoid derivatives, rehmannins A-D (1-4), along with an ionone derivative, 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone (5).

evidence.text_span: To address this gap, we focused on the pigmented tissues of R. glutinosa roots and successfully isolated four novel pigmented carotenoid derivatives, designated rehmannins A−D (1−4), along with an ionone derivative, 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone (5) (Figure 2).

thought.background: Novel compounds (rehmannins A-D) have been isolated.
thought.gap: Determine the molecular formula and planar structure of rehmannin A (1).
thought.decision: To determine the molecular formula, HR-ESI-MS was used. NMR data (1H-1H COSY, HMBC) were used to elucidate the planar structure.

outcome.observation: Molecular formula of rehmannin A (1) was established as C32H45NO4 by HR-ESI-MS. Planar assembly was resolved using NMR data, confirming linkages around C-9, C-13, C-18, and C-22, and the overall structure as shown in Figure 3.

evidence.text_span: The molecular formula of rehmannin A (1) was established as C32H45NO4 by HR-ESI-MS (m/z 508.3435 [M + H]+, calcd 508.3427), corresponding to 11 degrees of unsaturation. Analysis of the NMR data disclosed the presence of seven methyls ΔδC 24.6, 24.6, 30.0, 12.9, 12.8, 12.8, and 13.2), five methylenes (ΔδC 39.9, 19.4, 44.0, 43.5, and 62.0), one quaternary carbon (δC 47.0), 16 olefinic carbons (four quaternary alkenes at δC 135.3, 137.7, 137.5, and 131.4), three carbonyls (ΔδC 207.8, 203.3, and 169.7), and one amino group. Six proton spin systems, including C-2−C-3−C-4, C-7−C-8, C-10−C-11−C-12, C-14−C-15−C-16−C-17, and -NH-C-1′-C-2′, were identified from 1H-1H COSY correlations. The connectivity of these units was resolved using key HMBC correlations: from 27-Me to C-8/C-9/C-10; from 28-Me to C-12/C-13/C-14; from 29-Me to C-17/C-18/ C-19; and from 30-Me to C-21/C-22/C-23, confirming the linkages around C-9, C-13, C-18, and C-22, respectively. Additional HMBC correlations from 24-Me/25-Me ·δH 1.12) to C-1 (δC 47.0)/C-2 (δC 39.9)/C-6 ΔδC (ΔδC 203.3), from 26-Me (δH 2.05) to C-4 (δC 44.0)/C-5 Ω·δC ΔδC 207.8), from H-7 ·δH 6.74) to C-1 (δC 47.0)/C-6 ΔδC 203.3), and from H-1′ ·δH 3.39) to C-23 ΔδC 169.7) completed the planar assembly as shown in Figure 3.

thought.background: The planar structure of rehmannin A (1) is known.
thought.gap: Determine the geometry of the double bonds in rehmannin A (1).
thought.decision: To determine the double bond geometry, X-ray crystallographic analysis was performed.

outcome.observation: The geometry of all double bonds in rehmannin A (1) was assigned as the trans-configuration based on X-ray crystallographic analysis (Figure 3).

evidence.text_span: The geometry of all double bonds was assigned as the trans-configuration based on the X-ray crystallographic analysis of 1 (Figure 3).

thought.background: Novel compounds (rehmannins A-D) have been isolated.
thought.gap: Determine the molecular formula and planar structure of rehmannin B (2).
thought.decision: To determine the molecular formula, HR-ESI-MS was used. NMR data (HMQC, 1H-1H COSY, HMBC) were used to elucidate the planar structure.

outcome.observation: Molecular formula of rehmannin B (2) was assigned as C32H45NO5 by HR-ESI-MS. NMR analysis identified a structural fragment from C-9 to C-23-NH-C-1′-C-2′ identical to compound 1. Key HMBC correlations supported the formation of a cyclohexane ring and an ether linkage between C-4 and C-7, and C-5 and C-8, revealing a unique tricyclic heterocyclic system (Figure 3).

evidence.text_span: Rehmannin B (2) was assigned the molecular formula C32H45NO5 based on HR-ESI-MS (m/z 524.3386 [M + H]+, calcd 524.3376), indicating 11 degrees of unsaturation, the same as in compound 1. The HMQC spectrum revealed 14 olefinic carbon signals and one carbonyl signal, suggesting the presence of three rings in the structure. NMR analysis (Table 1) identified a structural fragment from C-9 to C-23-NH-C-1′-C-2′ identical to that in compound 1. Additional spin systems for C-2−C-3−C-4 and C-7−C-8 were established by 1H-1H COSY correlations. Key HMBC correlations from 24-Me/25- Me (δH 0.98/1.04) to C-1 (δC 37.1)/C-2 (δC 33.2)/C-6 (δC 83.0), from 26-Me (δH 1.23) to C-4 (δC 83.8)/C-5 (ΩδC 83.8)/ C-6 (δC 83.0), and from 6-OH (δH 5.02) to C-1 (δC 37.1)/C-5 (δC 83.8)/C-6 (δ 83.0)/C-7 (δC 80.7) supported the formation of a cyclohexane ring substituted with C-24/C-25 at C-1, C-26 at C-5, and a hydroxyl group at C-6 (Figure 3). An ether linkage between C-4 and C-7 was indicated by the HMBC correlation from H-4 to C-7, while the connection between C-5 and C-8 via an oxygen atom was supported by the correlation from H-8 to C-5, which was further confirmed by a long-range HMBC correlation from Me-26 (δH 1.23) to C-8 (δC 82.8δ). Based on these observations, a planar structure was established, revealing a unique tricyclic heterocyclic system (Figure 3).

thought.background: The planar structure of rehmannin B (2) is known.
thought.gap: Determine the relative configuration of rehmannin B (2).
thought.decision: To determine the relative configuration, ROESY spectrum analysis was performed.

outcome.observation: ROESY correlations suggested a β-orientation for H-4, 26-Me, 6-OH, and 24-Me, and an α-orientation for H-7, 25-Me, and 27-Me on the tricyclic core (Figure 3).

evidence.text_span: The relative configuration of 2 was deduced from the ROESY spectrum. The observed correlations of 24-Me (ΔδH 0.98)/6-OH (δH 5.02)/H-8 (δH 4.57) and 24-Me (δH 0.98)/ 26-Me (δH 1.23)/H-4 (δH 3.93)/H-10 (δH 6.33) collectively suggested a β-orientation for H-4, 26-Me, 6-OH, and 24-Me. Meanwhile, the ROESY correlations between H-7 (δH 4.07) and 25-Me (δH 1.04)/27·Me ·δH 1.73) indicated an α- orientation for these groups on the tricyclic core (Figure 3).

thought.background: The relative configuration of rehmannin B (2) is known.
thought.gap: Determine the absolute configuration of rehmannin B (2) using X-ray crystallography.
thought.decision: Attempt to grow X-ray crystals of compound (2).

outcome.observation: Several attempts to grow X-ray crystals of compound (2) were unsuccessful due to its instability, which caused it to readily degrade into different metabolites.

evidence.text_span: Although various methods were employed to grow X-ray crystals of compound (2), several attempts were unsuccessful due to its instability, which caused it to readily degrade into different metabolites.

thought.background: X-ray crystallography failed to determine the absolute configuration of rehmannin B (2) due to instability.
thought.gap: Determine the absolute configuration of rehmannin B (2) using an alternative method.
thought.decision: To determine the absolute configuration, the ECD spectrum recorded in MeOH was compared with the DT-DFT-calculated spectrum of 2.

outcome.observation: The calculated ECD spectrum of 2 matched well with the experimental spectrum, indicating the absolute configuration to be 4R, 5R, 6S, 7R, and 8S, consistent with 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone (5) isolated from the same plant (Figure 4).

evidence.text_span: The absolute configuration of 2 was determined by the comparison of the ECD spectrum recorded in MeOH and the DT-DFT-calculated spectrum of 2 at the cam-B3LYP/6-311++G(2d,p) level.4,5 The calculated ECD spectrum of 2 matched well with the experimental spectrum (Figure 4), which indicated the absolute configuration to be 4R, 5R, 6S, 7R, and 8S with the same stereochemistry of 5,6-dihydroxy-β- ionone (5) isolated from the same plant.

thought.background: Rehmannins C and D (3 and 4) share the same molecular formula as rehmannin B (2).
thought.gap: Determine the structural differences between rehmannins C and D (3 and 4) and rehmannin B (2).
thought.decision: NMR spectra were analyzed to identify significant differences, and ROESY correlations and isomerization shifts were used to assign double bond geometries.

outcome.observation: NMR spectra of 3 and 4 were nearly identical to 2, with significant differences in chemical shifts of certain olefinic protons, suggesting they were geometric isomers. The Z-configuration of the C-13/C-14 double bond in 3 was established by ROESY correlation and isomerization shifts. Similarly, the Z-geometry of the C-17/C-18 double bond in 4 was assigned by ROESY correlations and isomerization shifts. The absolute configurations of 3 and 4 were determined as 4R, 5R, 6S, 7R, and 8S, identical to 2, by ECD spectra analysis.

evidence.text_span: Rehmannins C and D (3 and 4) were determined to share the same molecular formula as rehmannin B (2) based on HR-ESI-MS data. Their NMR spectra were nearly identical to those of 2, with the only significant differences observed in the chemical shifts of certain olefinic protons, suggesting that they were geometric isomers. The Z-configuration of the C-13/C-14 double bond in 3 was established based on the ROESY correlation between H-28 and H-14 (Figure S6 and Figure S26), supported by isomerization shifts (Δδcis-trans) (Figure S5 and Table S6) and the characteristic chemical shift of C-28 Ω(δC) 20.5).6 Similarly, the Z-geometry of the C-17/C-18 double bond in 4 was assigned from the ROESY correlations of H-29/ H-17 and H-16/H-19 (Figure S6 and Figure S34), alongside analogous isomerization shifts (Δδcis-trans) and the chemical shift of C-29 (δC 20.2).6 The absolute configurations of 3 and 4 were determined as 4R, 5R, 6S, 7R, and 8S, identical to 2 by ECD spectra analysis (Figures S27 and S35).

thought.background: An ionone derivative (5) was isolated alongside rehmannins A-D.
thought.decision: Compare MS and NMR spectra of compound 5 with published data.

outcome.observation: Compound 5 was identified as 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone by comparison of its MS and NMR spectra with published data.

evidence.text_span: Compound 5 was identified as 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone by comparison of its MS and NMR spectra with published data7 (Figures S36−S38).

thought.background: The structures of rehmannins A-D (1-4) and ionone (5) are known, and they are colorful compounds.
thought.gap: Propose a biosynthetic pathway for these compounds.
thought.decision: Based on structural features and colorful characteristics, hypothesize that these compounds originate from carotenoid biosynthesis.

outcome.observation: A possible biosynthetic pathway was proposed (Scheme 1), starting from IPP/DMAPP forming β-carotene, which then undergoes oxidation and further modifications (amination, dehydration, cyclization) to yield rehmannins A-D and ionones.

evidence.text_span: Based on the structural features of 1−5 and colorful characteristics of rehmannins A−D (1−4), it suggested that these compounds might be originated from carotenoid biosynthesis.8 The possible biosynthetic pathway of rehmannins A−D (1−4) and ionone (5) was proposed (Scheme 1).

thought.background: Rehmannins A-D (1-4) and 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone (5) have been isolated and characterized.
thought.gap: Evaluate the biological activity of these compounds, specifically their anti-inflammatory effects.
thought.decision: To assess anti-inflammatory activity, the effects of compounds 1-5 on nitric oxide (NO) production were evaluated in LPS-stimulated RAW264.7 macrophages.

outcome.observation: At 100 μM, rehmannin C (3) and 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone (5) showed substantial NO inhibition (66.8% and 60.5%, respectively), comparable to quercetin (65.6% inhibition). Compounds 3 and 5 showed no cytotoxicity at 100 μM.

evidence.text_span: The effects of compounds 1−5 on nitric oxide (NO) production were evaluated in lipopolysaccharide (LPS)- stimulated RAW264.7 macrophages.10 At 100 μM, rehmannin C (3) and 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone (5) showed substantial NO inhibition 66.8% and 60.5%, respectively), comparable to quercetin11 65.6% inhibition) (Figure S3 and Table S2). Importantly, compounds 3 and 5 showed no cytotoxicity at 100 μM, underscoring their favorable safety profile and potential as a candidate for further drug development (Table S1).

validation.significance: This work reports the isolation and structural elucidation of four novel carotenoid derivatives, rehmannins A-D, from Rehmannia glutinosa. Rehmannins B-D possess a unique tricyclic ring system with five contiguous stereocenters and an amide group, a novel feature for carotenoids. Rehmannin C and 5,6-dihydroxy-β-ionone showed significant anti-inflammatory activity without cytotoxicity. The study also proposes a biosynthetic pathway and highlights the potential of R. glutinosa as a source of novel carotenoid-like molecules.

source.title: Rehmannins A-D, Anti-inflammatory Carotenoid Pigments from the Fresh Root of <i>Rehmannia glutinosa</i>.
source.authors: Zhong LW, Sun P, Liu KP, Zhang YT, Li K, Chen JL, Chen L, Yang J, Jia K, Ding G.
